The periorbital region shows chronic lymphedema and pigmentary changes, which are anatomically contiguous with a deep orbital hamartoma seen on CT.
Initial presentation included intermittent periorbital swelling, and subsequent imaging identified an incidental, non-enhancing hamartoma in the ipsilateral orbital apex.
While the orbital hamartoma is largely asymptomatic, it is accompanied by palpable firmness and irregularity of the adjacent periorbital soft tissues.
Serial photographs over five years document progressive periorbital tissue hypertrophy, with a stable, T2-hyperintense orbital hamartoma noted on all MRIs.
Contrast-enhanced studies show the hamartoma is confined to the orbit, yet it is associated with diffuse fatty infiltration of the periorbital subcutaneous layer.
The patient's periorbital abnormality, characterized by peau d'orange changes, is ipsilateral to a radiologically confirmed hamartoma within the orbital cavity.

template_lines: Urine organic acid analysis reveals elevated alpha-ketoglutarate alongside a marked increase in gamma-aminobutyric acid.
A follow-up metabolic panel shows persistently high urinary gamma-aminobutyric acid, while the previously elevated alpha-ketoglutarate has normalized.
During an episode of metabolic decompensation, gas chromatography-mass spectrometry confirmed a concurrent rise in both urinary alpha-ketoglutarate and gamma-aminobutyric acid.
Quantitative urine studies demonstrate an abnormal alpha-ketoglutarate concentration that correlates temporally with peaks in gamma-aminobutyric acid excretion.
Despite dietary intervention, serial urine collections continue to show increased gamma-aminobutyric acid and an abnormal alpha-ketoglutarate profile.
The initial metabolic screen was notable for increased urinary gamma-aminobutyric acid, though a repeat analysis later showed only isolated alpha-ketoglutarate abnormality.
Laboratory evaluation indicates co-existing elevations of alpha-ketoglutarate and gamma-aminobutyric acid in the urine without other significant carboxylic acid derangements.
Under fasting conditions, urine metabolite profiling shows a disproportionate increase in gamma-aminobutyric acid relative to the abnormal alpha-ketoglutarate levels.
The patient's urine organic acid profile is consistently abnormal, featuring both elevated gamma-aminobutyric acid and an irregular alpha-ketoglutarate concentration.
While urinary gamma-aminobutyric acid remains markedly increased on all samples, the alpha-ketoglutarate elevation appears only intermittently during metabolic stress.
